core_story.summary: How- ever, one of the challenges for achieving such generalization ability lies in the lack of available data. However, most of these works assume seen objects and unseen subjects, and the generalization capability of un- seen object geometry remains relatively under-explored. Our main insight lies in decoupling high-level temporal motion from fine-grained spatial hand-object interactions via a latent diffusion model coupled with a Grasping Variational Autoencoder (Grasp- VAE). In this paper, we propose LatentHOI, a novel approach de- signed to tackle the challenges of generalizing hand-object interaction synthesis to unseen objects. We conducted extensive experiments in an unseen-object setting on both single-hand grasping and bi-manual motion datasets, in- cluding GRAB, DexYCB †, and OakInk.

introduction_structure.main_message: Introduction Synthesizing 3D motion of humans is important for a variety of applications in computer graphics and robotics.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Contrasts prior work and emphasizes unresolved weaknesses.
introduction_structure.evidence_text:
1. Introduction
Synthesizing 3D motion of humans is important for a variety
of applications in computer graphics and robotics. Such
approaches can benefit 3D simulations, game design and
provide data for robotic imitation learning. Hand-object
interactions (HOI), which involve the complex motion of
hands engaging with diverse objects, present a unique and
challenging domain for 3D motion synthesis. Hands are dex-
terous and have many degrees of freedom; the contact state
with the object can change rapidly during interaction, and
minor perturbations—such as penetrations, temporal jitter,
and lack of contact—are highly noticeable. The grasping
pose also varies across different objects, given the subtle
nuances introduced by object shape and semantics.
To make hand-object interaction motion synthesis effec-
tive for downstream tasks, generative models need to be
capable of generalizing to unseen object geometry. How-
ever, one of the challenges for achieving such generalization
ability lies in the lack of available data. From a practical
point of view, capturing high-quality hand-object interac-
tion at scale is difficult and expensive, since fine-grained
hand-object interaction poses

only a handful of objects. For example, the largest real world
HOI dataset, GRAB [34], contains 1.3k motion sequences
across 51 objects, in contrast to the widely used human mo-
tion dataset AMASS [26], which includes 11.2k motion se-
quences spanning 344 subjects. Consequently, generalizing
across various objects becomes challenging when directly
applying standard generative model training pipelines used
in other domains.
With the general success of diffusion models in image
generation, recent work has successfully adopted diffusion
models for the generation of human motion [5, 38, 47] and
full-body object interaction [ 9, 22, 28, 40]. Despite the
progress, these works focus primarily on generating plau-
sible full-body motion rather than fine-grained hand-object
interaction. More recent and concurrent studies have ap-
plied diffusion models to hand-object interaction tasks such
as denoising [24], hand motion generation conditioned on
object input [21], and text-to-motion generation [2, 8, 11].
However, most of these works assume seen objects and
unseen subjects, and the generalization capability of un-
seen object ge

limitation_expression: However, most of these works assume seen objects and unseen subjects, and the generalization capability of un- seen object geometry remains relatively under-explored.
A straightforward adaptation of diffusion models to HOI that di- rectly models the joint distribution of hand-object sequences is prone to overfitting due to the limited data in available datasets.
method_expression: In this paper, we propose LatentHOI, a novel approach de- signed to tackle the challenges of generalizing hand-object interaction synthesis to unseen objects.

Quantitative and qualitative evaluations demonstrate that our method signif- icantly enhances the realism and physical plausibility of generated motions for unseen objects, both in single and bimanual manipulations, compared to the state-of-the-art.
To this end, we propose regularizing the generation process by decoupling global temporal hand and object motion from fine-grained spatial hand-object in- teractions.
The quantitative results indicate that our method can adapt better to unseen object shapes than the baselines across physics-based metrics.

In summary, our key contributions are as follows: • We propose LatentHOI, a novel framework aimed at en- hancing object generalization in 3D hand-object interac- tion motion generation. • Our model incorporates a novel design that decouples global motion from fine-grained grasping.
Our method demonstrates significantly reduced penetration while achieving a higher contact ratio on both unseen GRAB and OakInk objects, indicating more robust generalization to novel objects.
Discussion & Conclusion Limitations Despite our method achieving better general- ization compared to other approaches, it remains constrained by the limitations of the training data.
experiment_expression: We conducted extensive experiments in an unseen-object setting on both single-hand grasping and bi-manual motion datasets, in- cluding GRAB, DexYCB †, and OakInk.

Quantitative and qualitative evaluations demonstrate that our method signif- icantly enhances the realism and physical plausibility of generated motions for unseen objects, both in single and bimanual manipulations, compared to the state-of-the-art.
We conduct extensive experiments in an unseen-object setting on the GRAB [34], DexYCB [3] and OakInk [41] datasets.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
Current research on generating 3D hand-object interaction
motion primarily focuses on in-domain objects. Generaliza-
tion to unseen objects is essential for practical applications,
yet it remains both challenging and largely unexplored. In
this paper, we propose LatentHOI, a novel approach de-
signed to tackle the challenges of generalizing hand-object
interaction synthesis to unseen objects. Our main insight lies
in decoupling high-level temporal motion from fine-grained
spatial hand-object interactions via a latent diffusion model
coupled with a Grasping Variational Autoencoder (Grasp-
VAE). This configuration introduces regularization by en-
forcing a conditional dependency between spatial grasping
and temporal motion, as well as through the regularized
latent space for better generalization ability. We conducted
extensive experiments in an unseen-object setting on both
single-hand grasping and bi-manual motion datasets, in-
cluding GRAB, DexYCB †, and OakInk. Quantitative and
qualitative evaluations demonstrate that our method signif-
icantly enhances the realism and physical plausibility of
generated motions for unseen objects, both in single and
bimanual manipulations, compared to the state-of-the-art.
1 University of British Columbia; 2 Vector Institute for AI; 3 Canada
CIFAR AI Chair; 4 NSERC CRC Chair; 5 Meta Reality Lab, Redmond; 6
ETH Zurich, Switzerland;
* Corresponding author, work partially done during a internship at Meta
Reality Lab, Redmond. Contact: muchenli@cs.ubc.ca
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
Synthesizing 3D motion of humans is important for a variety
of applications in computer graphics and robotics. Such
approaches can benefit 3D simulations, game design and
provide data for robotic imitation learning. Hand-object
interactions (HOI), which involve the complex motion of
hands engaging with diverse objects, present a unique and
challenging domain for 3D motion synthesis. Hands are dex-
terous and have many degrees of freedom; the contact state
with the object can change rapidly during interaction, and
minor perturbations—such as penetrations, temporal jitter,
and lack of contact—are highly noticeable. The grasping
pose also varies across different objects, given the subtle
nuances introduced by object shape and semantics.
To make hand-object interaction motion synthesis effec-
tive for downstream tasks, generative models need to be
capable of generalizing to unseen object geometry. How-
ever, one of the challenges for achieving such generalization
ability lies in the lack of available data. From a practical
point of view, capturing high-quality hand-object interac-
tion at scale is difficult and expensive, since fine-grained
hand-object interaction poses usually require a much more
accurate annotation (compared, e.g., to human motion cap-
ture),which is further complicated by heavy occlusions. As a
result, most of the publicly available datasets for hand-object
†Data collection, analysis, and experiments involving DexYCB were con-
ducted at the University of British Columbia.
This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;
the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.
17416

interaction are relatively small-scale [3, 10, 20, 34] and span
only a handful of objects. For example, the largest real world
HOI dataset, GRAB [34], contains 1.3k motion sequences
across 51 objects, in contrast to the widely used human mo-
tion dataset AMASS [26], which includes 11.2k motion se-
quences spanning 344 subjects. Consequently, generalizing
across various objects becomes challenging when directly
applying standard generative model training pipelines used
in other domains.
With the general success of diffusion models in image
generation, recent work has successfully adopted diffusion
models for the generation of human motion [5, 38, 47] and
full-body object interaction [ 9, 22, 28, 40]. Despite the
progress, these works focus primarily on generating plau-
sible full-body motion rather than fine-grained hand-object
interaction. More recent and concurrent studies have ap-
plied diffusion models to hand-object interaction tasks such
as denoising [24], hand motion generation conditioned on
object input [21], and text-to-motion generation [2, 8, 11].
However, most of these works assume seen objects and
unseen subjects, and the generalization capability of un-
seen object geometry remains relatively under-explored. A
straightforward adaptation of diffusion models to HOI that di-
rectly models the joint distribution of hand-object sequences
is prone to overfitting due to the limited data in available
datasets. As we show in our experiments, this overfitting
results in poor generalization to unseen object shapes, lead-
ing to issues such as penetration errors, loss of tracking, and
physically implausible grasps.
In this paper, we aim to address these challenges and in-
troduce a novel model, LatentHOI, that enables the synthesis
of hand-object interactions for unseen object geometry based
on text descriptions. To this end, we propose regularizing
the generation process by decoupling global temporal hand
and object motion from fine-grained spatial hand-object in-
teractions. Specifically, we design a latent diffusion model
that generates the global motion of hands and objects as well
as a latent grasping code. We use this information as input to
a conditional V AE, dubbed GraspV AE, to generate detailed
hand poses. GraspV AE is a single frame model, that takes
as input hand position, object pose, and the latent code, and
outputs an articulated hand pose. This design offers sev-
eral significant benefits: 1. The two-stage pipeline enforces
conditional dependency from spatial grasping to temporal
motion to inherently regularize the model, thus generating
more plausible grasps for unseen objects. 2. Training the
diffusion model LatentHOI in the regularized latent space of
GraspV AE instead of directly in the pose space reduces the
risk of overfitting.
In our experiments, we compare our model against state-
of-the-art methods either designed for HOI motion genera-
tion [2, 11] or adapted from human motion generation lit-
erature [5, 38]. We conduct extensive experiments in an
unseen-object setting on the GRAB [34], DexYCB [3] and
OakInk [41] datasets. The quantitative results indicate that
our method can adapt better to unseen object shapes than
the baselines across physics-based metrics. Our qualitative
user study further demonstrates that our generated outputs
are more plausible and preferred over the baseline.
In summary, our key contributions are as follows:
• We propose LatentHOI, a novel framework aimed at en-
hancing object generalization in 3D hand-object interac-
tion motion generation.
• Our model incorporates a novel design that decouples
global motion from fine-grained grasping. This is achieved
through a latent diffusion model for motion generation and
a grasp V AE for grasp synthesis.
• We extensively evaluate our model on both bi-manual ma-
nipulation and single-hand grasping datasets, demonstrat-
ing its strong generalization ability to novel objects, both
quantitatively and qualitatively, compared to baselines.
raw_sections.method:
3. Method
Our methods contain two main modules: a grasp variational
auto encoder for encoding fine-grained hand and object
grasping pose, and a latent diffusion model for generating
high-level motion. We will first explain the intuition behind
such a design and then provide a description of each module.
raw_sections.experiments:
4. Experiments
We test our model’s capability of generalizing to new objects
on two motion generation setting: bi-manual manipulation
as well as single-hand grasping.
Datasets. For the bi-manual manipulation, we utilize the
popular GRAB [34] dataset along with novel objects from
the OakINK [41] dataset. In the GRAB dataset, we create an
object-based split, reserving 4 objects for the test set and us-
ing the remaining 47 objects for training. Focusing primarily
on the manipulation phase, we begin each motion sequence
from the first contact frame. We downsample the motion to
20 fps and pad or truncate all sequences to a maximum of
160 frames for training. For evaluation, objects in the unseen
split are used along with their intended motions, resulting
in a total of 17 (text prompt, object) pairs. To further eval-
uate the generalization capability of our model, we create
a challenging novel-object split from the OakInk dataset
[41]. We manually select 100 objects across 20 categories,

then pair these unseen object shapes with intents provided in
the GRAB dataset, resulting in a total of 212 (text prompt,
object) pairs for testing out-of-distribution generalization.
Since no motion data is available for training on this split,
we test the model using a checkpoint trained on the previ-
ously mentioned GRAB training set. Due to the same reason,
methods like IMoS, which rely on leading frames, are not
applicable for this split. The lack of training data and the
significant shape variation among selected objects make this
an especially challenging evaluation set. For the single-hand
grasping task, we use the DexYCB [3] dataset, creating an
object-based split by reserving 4 unseen objects out of 20
objects for the test set. We start from the first frame and
pad each motion sequence to a maxium of 96 frames. For
training GraspV AE, we use the same set of training data,
with the corresponding motion dataset split into individual
frames.
Implementation Details All experiments are conducted
on a single A100 GPU with 40GB of memory. For the
MDM[38], we use the same backbone architecture as our
latent diffusion model. For MLD [5], we employ the MLD-1
variant specified in the paper, along with the author-provided
model code and optimal hyperparameters. For both MDM
and MLD baselines, we apply the same training parameters
(e.g., learning rate, total epochs). Further details can be
found in the supplementary materials. For IMoS [ 11], we
use the values referenced in [ 8]. Text2HOI[ 2] is trained
on all objects of GRAB dataset and did not release their
training code. Therefore, we use their provided checkpoint

raw_sections.conclusion:
5. Discussion & Conclusion
Limitations Despite our method achieving better general-
ization compared to other approaches, it remains constrained
by the limitations of the training data. For instance, it strug-
gles to generalize to objects with sizes and geometries sig-
nificantly different from those in the training set, as demon-
strated by some failure cases provided in the supplementary
material. Additionally, it cannot generalize to unseen mo-
tion intents during prompting, simply because such motion
semantics are absent from the dataset. A promising direction
for future work is to leverage 3D motions reconstructed from
2D videos as additional signals for training, which could
help address these challenges.
Conclusions In this paper, we proposed to tackle the gen-
eralization issue of the hand motion generative model by
decomposing the generation of high-level temporal motion
from low-level grasping. The proposed method, latentHOI,
includes a latent diffusion module and a GraspV AE. We
conduct extensive evaluations, both qualitatively and quan-
titatively, using physics-based metrics, qualitative visual-
izations, and user surveys, demonstrating that our model
achieves strong generalization to novel objects.
